population: Adults aged 55+ with symptomatic hip OA
conclusion: Analgesic and physiotherapy management is low-cost but yields substantially fewer QALYs than surgical alternatives.

population: Patients on surgical waiting list opting for continued medical management
conclusion: Continued conservative management provides moderate quality of life; patients report persistent pain and functional limitation.

population: US adults with severe hip OA declining or ineligible for surgery
conclusion: Non-surgical management incurs ongoing costs with limited QALY accumulation over a 10-year horizon.

population: Community-dwelling adults with clinically diagnosed hip OA
conclusion: Conservative pathway generates acceptable QALYs at moderate cost in the Australian health system setting.

population: Adults with hip OA managed in primary and secondary care
conclusion: Exercise-based conservative management is the least costly pathway but accumulates fewer QALYs than surgical care over 10 years.
Primary THA is cost-effective at Finnish WTP thresholds and provides substantially more QALYs than conservative care.

population: Adult patients requiring primary hip replacement
conclusion: Primary hip replacement generates high QALY gains and is cost-effective across European health system thresholds.

population: Adults awaiting primary THA in NHS setting
conclusion: Well within NICE cost-effectiveness threshold; primary THA dominates prolonged conservative management.
Cost-effective at Canadian WTP of CAD 50 000/QALY with substantial QALY gains over conservative pathway.
Cost-effective at US WTP threshold of $50 000/QALY over a lifetime horizon.

indication: End-stage hip osteoarthritis — elevated dislocation risk
population: Adults undergoing THA at elevated dislocation risk
conclusion: Dual-mobility THA — high implant cost premium with marginal additional QALY gain. Consider excluding as outlier.

population: Adults requiring revision THA stratified by age and sex
conclusion: Revision THA restores function in patients with failed primary implants; ICER substantially higher than primary THA.

population: Patients requiring revision due to aseptic loosening
conclusion: Revision arthroplasty is costly but provides reasonable QALY outcomes in appropriately selected patients.

population: Adults undergoing revision THA in Swiss healthcare system
conclusion: Revision THA remains resource-intensive; authors note limited marginal QALY benefit versus retained primary implant.

population: Patients requiring two-stage revision for aseptic failure
conclusion: Aseptic revision THA is costly but provides sustained functional improvement over continued non-surgical management.

indication: Periprosthetic joint infection following primary THA
population: Patients requiring two-stage septic revision THA
conclusion: Septic revision THA carries substantially higher costs and lower QALY gains due to infection complications. Consider excluding as outlier.
